frontPage.headline: Suspended floors + Flood Zone 1 → check ventilation
frontPage.text: With a suspended floor on the EPC ("Suspended, no insulation (assumed)") and rivers & sea: Very Low; Flood Zone 1, prioritise underfloor ventilation and damp checks — Flood Zone 1 is not a free pass on timber voids.

frontPage.headline: Oil heating — tank and running-cost checks
frontPage.text: Main heating is recorded as "Boiler and radiators, oil" — budget for tank compliance, delivery access and running-cost volatility versus mains gas.

frontPage.headline: Certificate confirms a typical 1950–1966 fabric detail — worth checking
frontPage.text: Post-war cavity walls of this era were typically built with little or partial insulation. Typical for the era — and this certificate records: "Cavity wall, as built, partial insulation (assumed)". Check: Read the EPC walls line and confirm any later insulation.

frontPage.headline: Suspended floors + Flood Zone 1 → check ventilation
frontPage.text: With a suspended floor on the EPC ("Suspended, no insulation (assumed)") and rivers & sea: Very Low; Flood Zone 1, prioritise underfloor ventilation and damp checks — Flood Zone 1 is not a free pass on timber voids.

frontPage.headline: Certificate confirms a typical 1930–1949 fabric detail — worth checking
frontPage.text: Homes of this era commonly have cavity walls that were uninsulated when built unless later filled. Typical for the era — and this certificate records: "Cavity wall, as built, no insulation (assumed)". Check: Ask for evidence of cavity-wall insulation and check the EPC walls wording.

frontPage.headline: What is typical for a 1930–1949 home — and what to check
frontPage.text: Original clay drainage of this era is often nearing end of life. Check: Budget for a drainage CCTV survey before exchange.
